citation: Lolli L, Bahr R, Weston M, Whiteley R, Tabben M, Bonanno D, Gregson W, Chamari K, Di Salvo V, van Dyk N. No association between perceived exertion and session duration with hamstring injury occurrence in professional football. Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports. 2019;00:1-8. doi:10.1111/sms.13591
abstract: This exploratory study examined whether perceived exertion and session duration were associated with acute hamstring injury occurrence in professional football. Thirty outfield Qatar Stars League players with valid load and injury data across three seasons were included. The authors calculated average RPE, average session-RPE, cumulative minutes, cumulative session-RPE, and week-to-week changes across 7-, 14-, 21-, and 28-day windows, then used conditional logistic regression to compare each injured player's load before injury with that player's own non-injury periods. Corrected odds ratios for all candidate load variables were not practically important, with confidence intervals generally near null values. The authors concluded that perceived exertion and session duration remain useful for load management, but these measures alone were not empirically supported as hamstring injury risk determinants.
tldr: In pro soccer, RPE, session duration, and session-RPE over common weekly windows did not meaningfully distinguish hamstring injury periods from normal periods within the same player.
methods: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected Qatar Stars League injury surveillance and load-monitoring data from May 2015 to February 2018. Acute traumatic hamstring injuries causing more than 3 days lost were clinically confirmed; recurrent injuries were excluded from primary analysis. Session duration and Borg CR-10 RPE were collected after training and matches, with session-RPE calculated as duration x RPE. Exposure variables were summarized across 7-, 14-, 21-, and 28-day windows plus week-to-week changes. Conditional fixed-effects logistic regression modeled injury odds per 2 within-player SD increase, using each injured player as his own control.
findings:
- Thirty outfield players met inclusion criteria; median hamstring time loss was 18 days.
- Corrected odds ratios for average RPE, cumulative duration, average session-RPE, cumulative session-RPE, and week-to-week changes were not practically important across 7-, 14-, 21-, or 28-day windows.
- The 95% confidence intervals were close to null and did not support a clinically useful load-hamstring injury relationship for these internal-load measures.
- The study challenged broad load-injury claims when injury type, within-athlete comparison, and continuous modeling are handled more carefully.
- The authors still emphasized that load monitoring is valuable for player management, just not sufficient for type-specific hamstring risk prediction.
limitations: Small effective sample after large data loss; no formal a priori sample size was possible; RPE is a global perceptual measure and may miss local hamstring-specific stress; differential RPE and external-load variables such as sprinting were not primary exposures; match and training demands may need clearer separation; the cohort was mainly Middle-East professional soccer players, limiting transfer to NCAA American football.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, do not treat high session-RPE, total minutes, or week-to-week RPE changes as stand-alone hamstring injury alarms. Keep using RPE to manage total stress, communication, and recovery, but pair it with sprint exposure, high-speed running, position demands, eccentric strength, prior injury history, soreness reports, and technical/mechanical review when making hamstring-risk decisions.
athleteDev: The paper supports building robust hamstring preparation rather than simply chasing load thresholds. For skill players and specialists, athlete development should combine progressive sprint exposure, eccentric hamstring strength, high-speed mechanics, tissue capacity, and individualized recovery patterns while using RPE as one context marker.
rtp: Not an RTP study. Its RTP relevance is cautionary: session duration and RPE should not be used alone to decide return to high-speed running or full practice after hamstring injury. RTP decisions should include symptoms, strength, sprint mechanics, high-speed progression, prior injury context, and on-field tolerance.

citation: van Dyk N, Bahr R, Burnett AF, Verhagen E, von Tiggelen D, Witvrouw E. No association between rate of torque development and onset of muscle activity with increased risk of hamstring injury in elite football. Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports. 2018;00:1-11. doi:10.1111/sms.13224
abstract: This prospective cohort study tested whether hamstring rate of torque development and timing of muscle activity onset during isokinetic testing predicted future hamstring injury in elite male football players. Players from 18 Qatar Stars League teams completed annual periodic health evaluations across two seasons, including isokinetic knee flexion/extension testing with synchronized surface EMG of the biceps femoris and medial hamstrings. A total of 367 unique players contributed 514 player-seasons and sustained 65 hamstring injuries. There were no significant differences in muscle activity onset or rate of torque development between injured and uninjured limbs or between injured and uninjured players, and Cox models showed no meaningful association with future injury. The authors concluded that these measures are unsupported as screening tools for hamstring injury risk.
tldr: One-off isokinetic RTD and hamstring EMG onset testing did not identify elite soccer players who later sustained hamstring injuries.
methods: Prospective cohort across two Qatar Stars League seasons. Eligible adult players completed preseason or periodic health evaluation testing on a Biodex dynamometer with surface EMG over medial hamstrings and biceps femoris. Testing included concentric knee flexion/extension at 60 and 300 degrees/s and eccentric knee extension at 60 degrees/s. RTD was calculated over 30, 50, and 100 ms from contraction onset; EMG onset was detected relative to torque onset. Team medical staff prospectively recorded training/match exposure and clinically confirmed acute hamstring injuries. Analyses compared injured and uninjured limbs and used Cox regression for injury risk.
findings:
- The cohort included 367 unique players and 65 hamstring injuries.
- No significant differences were found in onset of muscle activity between injured and uninjured players for biceps femoris or medial hamstrings.
- No significant differences were found for RTD across contraction modes or early time windows; effect sizes were small, generally below d=0.3.
- Cox regression did not support EMG onset or RTD as future hamstring injury predictors.
- The authors interpreted prior retrospective deficits as more likely consequences of hamstring injury than pre-injury causes.
- Once-off periodic testing during the season was not valuable for identifying players at risk.
limitations: Although large for this topic, 65 injuries may still be underpowered for small associations; isokinetic seated testing does not replicate sprinting, cutting, or football-specific hamstring loading; dynamometer lag limits precision for EMG onset; fatigue and language/comprehension differences may have affected maximal efforts; repeated measures across a season may not reflect player status at injury time; results are from elite male soccer in Qatar and may not generalize to American football, female athletes, or youth players.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, do not invest in hamstring RTD plus surface EMG onset as a primary preseason screen for who will pull a hamstring. These tests may still help profile neuromuscular recovery or identify deficits after injury, but injury-risk decisions should prioritize a broader model: previous injury, sprint exposure, strength, fascicle/tendon qualities where available, fatigue, mechanics, and football workload.
athleteDev: Rapid force and coordinated hamstring activation still matter for sprinting and deceleration development, but this paper argues against using one lab snapshot as a risk label. Build RTD through progressive sprinting, resisted sprinting, eccentric work, hip extension strength, and high-speed skill exposures, then evaluate whether athletes can express those qualities in football-specific tasks.
rtp: Directly relevant to hamstring rehabilitation. Deficits in RTD and activation may be post-injury consequences, so restoring rapid torque production and biceps femoris function can be useful during RTP. However, passing a once-off RTD or EMG test should not be treated as proof of low recurrence risk without sprint progression and field-based criteria.

citation: Bellinger PM, Ferguson C, Newans T, Minahan CL. No Influence of Prematch Subjective Wellness Ratings on External Load During Elite Australian Football Match Play. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. 2020;(Ahead of Print):1-7. doi:10.1123/ijspp.2019-0395
abstract: This retrospective observational study examined whether prematch subjective wellness influenced external match load in elite Australian football. Thirty-four elite male AFL players from one team completed wellness questionnaires before, on, and after matches across two seasons, producing 460 match observations and 1840 wellness entries. Wellness items included mood, energy, stress, leg heaviness, muscle soreness, sleep quality, hours slept, and total wellness, expressed as within-player z scores. GPS and accelerometer data quantified relative distance, high-speed distance, PlayerLoad, and directional PlayerLoad metrics. Linear mixed models showed trivial effects of match-day wellness on subsequent external match load. Match result and PlayerLoad in the anteroposterior vector were associated with next-day wellness, suggesting match context and movement demands affected perceived recovery more than prematch wellness affected match output.
tldr: In AFL match play, players' prematch wellness scores did not meaningfully predict how much external load they produced, likely because normal 6- to 8-day match cycles restored wellness by game day.
methods: Retrospective two-season AFL study in 34 elite male players with at least 20 AFL matches and at least 75% match time in included games. Players completed mobile wellness questionnaires at -3, -2, -1, match day, and +1 to +3 days around matches. GPS at 10 Hz and accelerometers at 100 Hz measured relative distance, high-speed distance, total PlayerLoad, PlayerLoadSLOW, 2D PlayerLoad, and anteroposterior, mediolateral, and vertical PlayerLoad per minute. Linear mixed models evaluated wellness, match result, location, and between-match recovery duration, with player and match as random effects.
findings:
- Match-day total wellness and individual wellness items had trivial effects on all external match-load metrics.
- Wellness was lowest on the day after matches and generally improved toward match day across 6-, 7-, and 8-day microcycles.
- A win was associated with a 0.30 z-score increase in next-day subjective wellness.
- Each 1-unit increase in PlayerLoadAP per minute was associated with a 0.15 z-score reduction in next-day wellness.
- Higher high-speed distance was associated with higher next-day wellness, possibly reflecting less contested or less physically damaging matches.
- The authors still recommended wellness monitoring using individual-relative changes rather than raw group thresholds.
limitations: Single elite AFL team; retrospective design; the wellness questionnaire was practical but not rigorously validated for reliability and validity; training load prescription was not controlled; AFL match cycles and substitution rules differ from college American football; wellness had little variation by match day, so findings may not apply to short turnarounds, travel stress, heat, illness, or congested schedules.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, a low or high wellness score should not automatically imply an athlete will produce less game load. Wellness is still useful for identifying recovery problems and guiding the practice week, but game-day role, competitive context, position rotation, and tactical demands may override self-reported readiness. Use individual z-score changes and follow up with conversation rather than rigid cutoffs.
athleteDev: Daily wellness can help teach athletes to connect sleep, soreness, stress, and recovery behaviors to the training week. It is most actionable when paired with GPS, practice intensity, lift outputs, and staff notes, especially for developing players learning how to recover between high-contact or high-speed exposures.
rtp: Wellness should support, not replace, RTP decision-making. A returning athlete who reports good match-day wellness may still need objective exposure progression, strength testing, position-specific movement, and contact tolerance. Conversely, poor wellness after a game or practice can flag the need to slow the next progression.

citation: Smith NA, Bourne MN, Franettovich Smith MM, Barrett RS, Hides JA. Nordic strength and history of hamstring injury in Australian Football League players. Physical Therapy in Sport. 2022;57:11-16. doi:10.1016/j.ptsp.2022.06.006
abstract: This cross-sectional retrospective study compared Nordic hamstring strength in AFL players with and without a history of hamstring injury. One hundred twenty-four players from three clubs were recruited, with exclusions for other lower-limb or lumbopelvic injuries. Players completed a self-reported hamstring injury history questionnaire and bilateral Nordic Hamstring Exercise strength testing on a Nordbord. After grouping, 43 players with complete data had no prior hamstring injury and 15 had complete data with prior hamstring injury. Nineteen hamstring injuries were reported overall. Nordic strength, relative strength, and between-limb asymmetry did not differ significantly between previously injured and uninjured players. Injury number, recency, and severity also did not significantly influence strength. The authors concluded that these elite AFL players did not show residual Nordic strength deficits after prior hamstring injury.
tldr: In modern AFL players, prior hamstring injury did not automatically mean lower Nordic strength or meaningful asymmetry, possibly reflecting stronger rehabilitation and prevention practices.
methods: Cross-sectional retrospective study in AFL players from three clubs. Players completed self-reported injury history, including injured leg, time since injury, return-to-play time, and matches missed. Prior hamstring injury was defined as an acute non-contact hamstring injury during AFL training or play, diagnosed by club medical staff, causing at least one missed match. Nordic strength was assessed bilaterally during three maximal Nordic Hamstring Exercise repetitions on a Nordbord. Analyses compared no-injury and hamstring-injury groups, then injury subgroups by single versus multiple injuries, less than or equal to 1 year versus more than 1 year since injury, and less than or equal to 3 versus more than 3 matches missed.
findings:
- No significant Nordic strength differences were found between uninjured players and players with prior hamstring injury in absolute or body-mass-relative terms.
- No significant asymmetry differences were found between groups.
- Previously injured limbs were not significantly weaker than contralateral uninjured limbs.
- Number of prior injuries, recency of injury, and severity based on matches missed did not significantly affect Nordic strength or asymmetry.
- Previously injured players were older, but age adjustment did not change the strength findings.
- The authors suggested improved hamstring strengthening and rehabilitation practices in AFL may explain higher strength values and lack of residual deficits.
limitations: Small number of players with complete prior-injury data and very small subgroup sizes, increasing type II error risk; hamstring injury history relied on self-report and recall; training and rehabilitation history were not controlled; data came from elite AFL players and may not transfer to lower-level athletes; testing occurred across four sites with different assessors and devices, although Nordbord reliability is generally high.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, do not assume an athlete with prior hamstring injury is currently weak on Nordic testing. Measure it. If Nordic strength and asymmetry are normal, the remaining risk may sit elsewhere: sprint exposure history, tissue architecture, fatigue, mechanics, position demands, prior injury recency, or incomplete football-specific reconditioning.
athleteDev: This supports keeping Nordic strength as a standing development quality for speed-position athletes, not only as a rehab tool. Strong team norms and regular exposure may reduce persistent deficits after injury, but Nordics should be one part of a broader posterior-chain and sprint-development plan.
rtp: Useful for RTP benchmarking after hamstring injury. Athletes should restore Nordic strength and symmetry toward individual or position norms, but normal Nordbord values do not guarantee readiness for maximal sprinting, reactive cuts, or return to contact. Field progression remains essential.

citation: Havira P, Brewer BW, Van Raalte JL, Jennison D, France TJ III. Not much I in Team: Content analysis of pregame speeches in American football. International Journal of Sports Science & Coaching. 2023;1-8. doi:10.1177/17479541231165135
abstract: This descriptive content-analysis study examined the content, delivery, and context of American football pregame speeches. Researchers identified 127 complete, audible, visually clear speeches on YouTube, including 77 high school, 37 intercollegiate, and 13 cinematic speeches. Speeches were transcribed, coded with a custom descriptive/verbal content system, and analyzed with LIWC2015 software. Actual speeches typically lasted about two minutes, were usually delivered by coaches in locker rooms, and commonly included repeated words, questioning, game instructions, references to opponents, team past, and the physical environment. Compared with natural speech, football pregame speeches used more analytical, confident, collective, motivational, emotional, and future-oriented language, with relatively less first-person singular language. The authors concluded that pregame speeches are a distinct communication form tailored to the moment before competition.
tldr: Real football pregame speeches are short, collective, confident, emotionally charged, and instruction-linked, with more 'we/you/team' language than ordinary speech.
methods: Video clips of American football pregame speeches were identified on YouTube using the search term 'football pregame speech' on January 1, 2018. Complete, audible, visually available speeches were downloaded and transcribed; incomplete, inaudible, or visually unclear clips were excluded. A custom coding system captured 16 descriptive and verbal content variables such as speaker, setting, opponent references, game instructions, questioning, repeated phrases, metaphor, and anecdote. LIWC2015 quantified analytical thinking, clout, authenticity, emotional tone, pronouns, emotion, drives, time focus, and swear words. Chi-square tests, MANOVAs, and one-sample t-tests compared actual speeches with cinematic speeches, high school with intercollegiate speeches, and actual speeches with natural speech norms.
findings:
- Actual pregame speeches averaged about 123 seconds and were most often delivered by coaches in locker rooms.
- Common actual-speech features included repeated words, game instructions, questioning, opponent references, team-past references, and environmental references.
- Actual speeches differed from natural speech on 14 of 17 LIWC variables, indicating a distinct communication style.
- Compared with natural speech, actual speeches showed higher analytical thinking, clout, we, you, they, negative emotion, drives, and future focus, and lower I, he/she, compare, positive emotion, and emotional tone.
- High school and intercollegiate speeches were broadly similar, though intercollegiate speeches used more we, positive emotion, negative emotion, present focus, and swear words.
- Actual and cinematic speeches were similar on LIWC variables, but actual speeches included more football- and team-specific references and fewer metaphors.
limitations: Speeches were limited to public YouTube clips and may not represent all team environments; the presence of cameras may have changed speaker behavior; findings may not generalize to professional, youth, female, or non-football teams; the study was descriptive and did not measure athlete motivation, physiology, execution, or performance outcomes; no causal claim can be made about what speech style improves play.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the study supports pregame communication that is brief, collective, confident, and connected to the opponent and game plan. The useful takeaway is not theatrical hype, but a clear blend of emotional activation, tactical reminders, shared identity, and concrete behavioral cues. Sport science staff can align readiness language with coaching messages so athletes hear one coherent theme before competition.
athleteDev: Developing athletes may benefit from consistent, team-oriented language that links preparation, identity, and immediate execution. Captains and position leaders can be coached to use short collective cues, repeat key behaviors, and avoid overlong or unfocused messages that dilute attention before play.
rtp: Not an RTP study. Indirectly, the communication findings can help staff frame return-to-play messaging so returning athletes feel integrated into the team without being overloaded. RTP communication should be specific, calm, and role-linked rather than purely emotional.
